scope: First-pass cross-omics mapping groups for the three LiverTx-OmicsDB demonstrator use cases.
notes: These mappings are question-oriented integration groups, not claims of direct mechanistic causality.
A mapping group can be supported by mixed evidence grades; users should inspect the linked evidence tables before treating a group as validated.
Taxon links are left empty for these three demonstrators because the first wave focuses on tissue, blood, serum, and donor-organ contexts rather than gut-liver microbiome use cases.

mapping_groups.label: IFN-gamma chemokine and cytotoxic rejection program
mapping_groups.biological_theme: Adaptive immune rejection with interferon signaling and cytotoxic effector activity.
mapping_groups.interpretation_scope: This is the cleanest current rejection-leaning anchor in the first demonstrator set.
mapping_groups.main_caveat: This group is still dominated by a single primary biopsy dataset and should not be presented as a clinically validated rejection classifier.

mapping_groups.label: Heme metabolism and oxidative-stress response
mapping_groups.biological_theme: Stress-response biology that may rise in injury contexts and can overlap with inflammatory graft dysfunction.
mapping_groups.interpretation_scope: Useful as a bridge between rejection-associated serum proteomics and broader graft-stress biology.
mapping_groups.main_caveat: Current support is a small discovery-set serum layer and should be treated as marker context, not definitive biology.

mapping_groups.label: Endothelial injury and acute-phase inflammation
mapping_groups.biological_theme: Innate inflammatory and endothelial perturbation signatures linked to early graft injury and postreperfusion dysfunction.
mapping_groups.interpretation_scope: This is the main proteomic counterweight to the TCMR-centric transcriptome signal and helps keep injury distinct from rejection.
mapping_groups.main_caveat: The cohort captures early AKI-linked graft injury, which is relevant but not identical to all early injury states in clinical liver transplantation.

mapping_groups.label: Coagulation, lipid transport, and metabolic stress
mapping_groups.biological_theme: Systemic graft dysfunction context reflected in serum proteins related to coagulation and lipid handling.
mapping_groups.interpretation_scope: This group is useful for explaining why blood evidence may reflect broader graft physiology rather than rejection alone.
mapping_groups.main_caveat: Serum proteins in this group are biologically informative but are not specific enough to separate injury and rejection on their own.

use_cases.title: Donor liver quality and graft viability

mapping_groups.label: Hepatocyte synthetic and xenobiotic-metabolism competence
mapping_groups.biological_theme: Preserved hepatocyte identity and metabolic competence in donor organs.
mapping_groups.interpretation_scope: This is the primary donor-quality transcriptomic axis and should anchor donor-organ interpretation in V1.
mapping_groups.main_caveat: Selection-status labels reflect donor-organ acceptance decisions rather than downstream recipient graft survival.

mapping_groups.label: Stress-response and extracellular-matrix remodeling
mapping_groups.biological_theme: Oxidative stress and matrix remodeling that may mark suboptimal donor-organ biology.
mapping_groups.interpretation_scope: This group provides the stress-and-fibrosis side of donor-quality interpretation that complements hepatocyte competence markers.
mapping_groups.main_caveat: A compact marker set is useful for interpretation, but full donor-quality module scoring still needs to be formalized.

mapping_groups.label: Biliary and epithelial viability surface program
mapping_groups.biological_theme: Proteins linked to biliary viability and donor-organ surface remodeling during machine perfusion.
mapping_groups.interpretation_scope: This is the main proteomic viability layer for donor quality and machine-perfusion biology in the current build.
mapping_groups.main_caveat: These signals speak most directly to donor-organ viability during machine perfusion and should not be overtranslated into recipient-outcome claims.

mapping_groups.label: Interferon and inflammatory blood-monitoring program
mapping_groups.biological_theme: Inflammatory and interferon-linked blood programs that may reflect systemic graft perturbation.
pathway_context: Type I and II interferon signaling
mapping_groups.interpretation_scope: This is the clearest blood-RNA monitoring axis in the current database.
mapping_groups.main_caveat: These markers support longitudinal monitoring but do not yet prove biopsy-equivalent rejection classification.

mapping_groups.label: Tolerance and immune homeostasis program
mapping_groups.biological_theme: Blood-based immune regulation and withdrawal-risk stratification.
mapping_groups.interpretation_scope: This group should anchor the tolerance side of the blood demonstrator and remain separate from generic injury-monitoring claims.
mapping_groups.main_caveat: Current proteomic support is still a narrow marker layer rather than a full sample-level tolerance proteome.

mapping_groups.label: Serum protein stress, transport, and renal/hepatic complication context
mapping_groups.biological_theme: Circulating proteins reflecting graft dysfunction, complication context, and systemic stress rather than a single transplant phenotype.
mapping_groups.interpretation_scope: This is the main serum-proteomics bridge layer for the blood demonstrator, but it mixes multiple complication contexts intentionally.
mapping_groups.main_caveat: These proteins provide broad monitoring context and should not be treated as a single coherent rejection-specific serum signature.

mapping_groups.label: Energy, amino-acid, and lipid metabolism context
mapping_groups.biological_theme: Serum metabolite shifts that may help separate inflammatory, biliary, and metabolic graft pathology contexts.
mapping_groups.interpretation_scope: This group gives the blood demonstrator a true non-transcriptomic, non-proteomic layer tied to post-transplant pathology context.
mapping_groups.main_caveat: These metabolites come from a strong pathology-context cohort, but they still require independent validation before clinical monitoring claims.
